coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence turn-on immunoassay of endocrine diethyl phthalate in daily supplies using red fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Biru
Li, Lei
Hu, Yue
Liu, Bolin
Guo, Ming
Zhang, Qi
Yang, Qianqian
Zhang, Mingcui
coredata.dc:description: Diethyl phthalate (DEP), as the environmental endocrine, would interfere with people’s endocrine system and has greatly harm in health. Here a sensitivity, eco-friendly fluorescence immunoassay technique was established based on “turn-on” red carbon dots (R-CDs) conjugated anti-DEP antibody (Ab) as the immunoprobe for the detection of DEP in daily supplies. The R-CDs will “turn-on” fluorescence when it in formamide solution, due to the change of the electron distribution on the surface and electronic charge transfer of the red carbon dots. The use of “turn-on” R-CDs as immunoprobe effectively increased the sensitivity of the immunoassay. Moreover, the R-CDs conjugated anti-DEP Ab without any additional embellishment. Under the optimal conditions, a good linear was established with the DEP concentration range of 5 × 10−2 ng/mL–10 ng/mL, the low limit of detection (LOD) of the proposed method was 0.0056 ng/mL. This project has satisfactory accuracy with recoveries ranging from 96.0% to 115.1%. Our research provides an accurately, simply and sensitivity strategy to detect the content of environmental endocrine DEP in daily supplies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence immunoassay
Red carbon dots
Diethyl phthalate
Fluorescence probe
Antibody
Formamide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of hapten and coating antigen
Recognition of hapten and coating antigen
Characterization of carbon dots coupled antibody
Theory of fluorescent immunoassay based on R-CDs as labels
Analytical properties about the competitive immunoassay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the R-CDs. (a) TEM image of R-CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and Exmax, Emmax fluorescence intensity of R-CDs, the inset FL spectroscopy of the R-CDs was under the 530 nm light. (c) EDS spectrum of R-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectra and (e) Raman spectra of R-CDs. (f) XRD patterns of R-CDs.
Characterization of the R-CDs and R-CDs-anti-DEP Ab. (a) UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and (b) FL spectroscopy of R-CDs and R-CDs-anti-DEP Ab. (c) FT-IR spectra of R-CDs and R-CDs-anti-DEP Ab.

Optimization of experimental conditions. (a) The concentration of antigen antibody reaction and (b) the incubation time of specific binding of antigen and antibody. (c) Block time for competitive immunoreaction. (d) pH value of buffer.
The corresponding standard curves of (a) fluorescence immunoassay and (b) ELISA.
(a) Synthesis of R-CDs. (b) Schematic illustration of R-CDs based immunoassay for DEP detection.
Recovery and precision of the fluorescent immunoassay (n = 3, 100-fold dilution) and GC-MS/MS (n = 3) in spiked samples.
Comparison of the present method with reported immunoassays for the detection of DEP.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: FPIA was defined as fluorescence polarization immunoassay. b FITC refers to the fluorescein isothiocyanate. c TEFIA is the abbreviation of Time-resolved fluoroimmunoassay. d HRP refers to the horseradish peroxidase. e BA-ELISA is the immunoassay based on the Avidin and Horseradish peroxidase labeled avidin. f SA-HRP refers to the streptavidin-HRP.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence turn-on immunoassay of endocrine diethyl phthalate in daily supplies using red fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Anhui Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing, Laboratory of Biosensing and Bioimaging, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Anhui Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing, Laboratory of Biosensing and Bioimaging, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Provincial Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Hefei, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Provincial Center for Disease Control and Prevention

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Anhui Provincial Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Hefei, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu 241000, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: “Turn on” fluorescence immunoassay of endocrine diethyl phthalate.

Diethyl phthalate (DEP), as the environmental endocrine, would interfere with people’s endocrine system and has greatly harm in health. Here a sensitivity, eco-friendly fluorescence immunoassay technique was established based on “turn-on” red carbon dots (R-CDs) conjugated anti-DEP antibody (Ab) as the immunoprobe for the detection of DEP in daily supplies. The R-CDs will “turn-on” fluorescence when it in formamide solution, due to the change of the electron distribution on the surface and electronic charge transfer of the red carbon dots. The use of “turn-on” R-CDs as immunoprobe effectively increased the sensitivity of the immunoassay. Moreover, the R-CDs conjugated anti-DEP Ab without any additional embellishment. Under the optimal conditions, a good linear was established with the DEP concentration range of 5 × 10−2 ng/mL–10 ng/mL, the low limit of detection (LOD) of the proposed method was 0.0056 ng/mL. This project has satisfactory accuracy with recoveries ranging from 96.0% to 115.1%. Our research provides an accurately, simply and sensitivity strategy to detect the content of environmental endocrine DEP in daily supplies.

ce:section.ce:para: Diethyl phthalate (DEP), one in the family of phthalic acid esters (PAEs). It is a plasticizer that is commonly used to promote flexibility and long-term durability in the plastic, automotive, lubricant, textile, cosmetics, and pesticide industries [1,2]. The existing studies have found that PAEs can migrate to the environment by volatilizing or leaching, therefore DEP can be detected in industrial wastewater, rivers, and foodstuff [3–5]. As the environmental endocrine, it has a serious impact on people’s health. Not only can it increase the breast cancer of woman and affect the sperm survival rate due to the broadly exposed to human [6–8], but it will affect the health of newborns and babies [9,10]. According to reports, the presence of esters can be detected in the feces of fetuses, infants and adults, and infants are exposed to higher levels of PAEs than adults [11,12]. Without doubt, the presence of DEP in daily consumables is more harmful to children. Above all, it is vital to devise a technique for detecting DEP in the products of our daily supplies.

Gas chromatography (GC) [13], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [14,15], gas chromatography-flame ionization detection [16], gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [17] and gas chromatography-electron capture detector (GC-ECD) [18] are some exiting of analytical method used to monitor DEP. Nevertheless, these approaches of using equipment are expensive and time-wasting, and they necessitate complex sample pretreatments for purification and enrichment, as well as highly trained people. Enzyme-linked immunoassay (ELISA), as a very classic immunoassay (also called golden immunoassay), is frequently used in the detection of phthalic acid esters [19]. As an alternative method in detection, immunoassays with low cost, high throughput, high sensitivity, and simple sample preparation have been developed for the detection of PAEs in ambient and practical samples. In traditional ELISA, the enzyme substrate generates colored products catalyzed by horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [20] or alkaline phosphatase (ALP) [21].

To improve the sensitivity of ELISA, fluorescence immunoassay (FIA) has been developed based on traditional ELISA assays. Organic fluorescent dyes, metallic nanoparticles/nanoclusters [22–24], quantum dots (QDs) [25] and rare earth up-conversion nanomaterials [26] as classical fluorescent nanomaterials, which are the common fluorescent materials for fluorescence immunoassay. Currently, they have inherent flaws that limit their practical applicability in immunoassay. For instance, heavy metal semiconductor quantum dots have certain toxicity and poor stability, which limit their application in immunoassays; on the other hand, the organic fluorescent dyes, such as rhodamine, fluorescein, coumarin also not the good choice for fluorescence immunoassays because of their poor solubility, severe photobleaching, and poor biocompatibility. At this time, a new type of fluorescent probe was found to be crucial for fluorescence immunoassay. Carbon dots, as a 0-dimensional material less than 10 nm, its synthesis method is simple, green and environmentally friendly [27]. Carbon dots are often used for bioimaging, ion detection and pesticide analysis [28–31]. On top of that, they have been utilized in immunoassays, western blots, and immunohistochemistry for they can interact with particular biological molecules such as antibodies, peptides, and adapters [32,33].

In this study, based the “turn-on” red carbon dots (R-CDs) as probe, a new fluorescence immunoassay was established. R-CDs has great penetrating power and little background interference, as the turn-on type fluorescence probe can reduce the interference between the environment and the actual sample medium which will improve the sensitivity of the immunoassay. In our strategy, R-CDs and anti-DEP antibody (anti-DEP Ab) were conjugated to set up one stable and environment-friendly fluorescent immune probes. The nanoprobe showed good fluorescence properties and good biocompatibility. More importantly, the R-CDs was capable of directly labeled the anti-DEP Ab without any other modifications. We can quantitative analysis the concentration of DEP in the samples by virtue of the specific binding of antigens and antibody. “Turn-on” R-CDs as labels for fluorescence immunoassay has lower detection limit than the classic ELISA which on the account of enzymes. In a word, this proposed fluorescence immunoassay provides a simple, sensitive and reliable strategy to detect DEP in the products of our daily life.

Anhydrous citric acid (CA) and 4-nitrophthalic acid were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Biological Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine (EDA), formamide, benzene, ethanol, standard for diethyl phthalate (DEP) and the analogues, dioctyl phthalate (DOP), dimethyl phthalate (DMP) were supplied by Macklin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dicyclohexyl phthalate (DCHP), dibutyl phthalate (DBP), concentrated sulfuric acid, sodium hydroxide, urea, concentrated hydrochloric acid and zinc powder were got from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). Ovalbumin (OVA) and Bovine serum albumin (BSA) were from purchased Bomei Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Hefei, China). 96-well microtiter plates were obtained from Corning Life Sciences (New York, USA). Supporting Information described the buffers used in the project.

The fluorescence intensity in the 96-well polystyrene plate was detected Synergy HT microplate reader (Synergy, USA) at 550/615 nm. UV − vis absorption spectra measured on the U-3900 Spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs was received on the F-4700 FL Spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The decay time and absolute quantum yield of the red carbon dots was obtained on the FLS-1000 FL Spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, US). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were carried out using a Hitachi HT7700 microscopy (Hitachi, Japan). Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) spectrum obtained by the screen electron microscopy (SEM) performed on HT-8100 (Hitachi, Japan). X-ray powder diffraction apparatus (XRD) obtained from the D8 Advance (Bruker, Germany). Raman spectrum was detected from the inVia Raman spectrometer (Renishaw, UK), and the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on IR-21 FT-IR spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with the KBr pellet technique. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrograph (Thermo Scientific, USA). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed on a Compact laser light scattering instrument (ALV/CGS-8F, Germany). Preparation of Ultrapure water (18.25 ΜΩ cm-1) by Millipore purification system (PSDK, Beijing). Using Agilent 7890A-7000B GC-MS/MS (Agilent, USA) to confirm the reliability of fluorescence immunoassay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of hapten and coating antigen
ce:section.ce:para: DEP as a small molecule has no immunogenicity, hence, itmustbelinkedtothecarrierproteintoachievethispurpose. The following processes were used to make several haptens with an ester, nitro, or amine group. The detail synthesis strategy of 4-nitrophthalate (4-DENP), diethyl 4-aminophthalate (4-DEAP) were modified by the pervious method [34] and the conjugation of 4-DEAP coupled to OVA via amino diazotization linkage was used as the coating antigen [35,36].

R-CDs were synthesized using a procedure modified from the reported method [37,38]. In detail, in order to product a clear mixed liquor, CA and EDA were dissolved in formamide. Then use Teflon to accommodate the mixture, maintained at 180 °C for 4 h for solvothermal reaction. The resulting product solution was precipitated by adding 150 mL of acetone overnight at room temperature after the reaction was gently cooled to room temperature. After that, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min), and then the precipitate was washed several times with acetone and 10% (v/v) ethanol/acetone washing mixture and dried in vacuum. Finally, dark powder of R-CDs was procured and stored at 4 °C for later use.

In this experiment, glutaraldehyde one-step method was used to conjugate carbon dot with antibodies [39]. At first, the purification R-CDs dissolved for later use. Then 200 µL of 2.7 mg/mL anti-DEP Ab solution (dissolved in 0.01 mol/L PBS, pH = 7.4) was gently added to the red carbon solution, and the mixed liquor stirring for 5 min at room temperature. After that, slowly added 200 µL of 25% glutaraldehyde solution to the mixture solution, the reaction reacted 2 h in the dark. The R-CDs coupled to the anti-DEP Ab due to the formation of the Schiff base. The synthesized R-CDs labeled anti-DEP Ab was isolated by dialysis against 0.01 mol/L PBS for 2 days to remove the excess R-CDs and salts with several buffer changes. The purified solution was collected and stored at 4 °C for later use.

Firstly, 4-DEAP-OVA (coating antigen) in coating buffer solution (100 μL) was added into each well of the 96-well microtiter plates for incubation at 4 °C overnight. After that, the solutions were discarded and the PBST buffer was added into each well and washed for three times to remove the unbound coating antigen. To prevent nonspecific adsorption, added 1.5% OVA (200 μL) into each well. After being incubated for 1 h, difference concentration DEP standard solution (50 μL) and R-CDs labeled anti-DEP Ab (50 μL) were added into each well following the subsequent washing steps, respectively. After 2 h of incubation at 37 °C, washed with PBST three times, then 100 μL of formamide was added into each well, measured the fluorescence of the solution at 550 nm by the Synergy HT microplate reader.

The actual sample was purchased from Auchan Supermarket (Wuhu, China). The actual samples of paper and plastic were identified as diethyl phthalate negative by GC-MS/MS. The extraction of phthalates from samples referenced the National Standard of the People's Republic of China (GB/T 37860-2019, GB 31604.30-2016) and modified slightly. Briefly, the sample to be tested was pulverized, and accurately weighed 0.5 g (accurate to 0.0001 g) into a 100 mL conical bottle with stopper. Then, twenty mL n-hexane was added to each test tube and perform ultrasonic extraction at 40 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, the extract was filtered with an organic phase microporous filter membrane (0.22 μm), and the filtrate was distilled under reduced pressure at 40 °C [40]. After the solution was concentrated less than 1 mL, the volume was made up to 1 mL with absolute ethanol. At last, the resulting solution collect in glass bottles until future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition of hapten and coating antigen
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. S1a), FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1b) and HNMR (CDCl3) spectrum (Fig. S2) proved 4-DENP was successfully synthesis. And 4-DEAP presents two characteristic absorption peaks in the UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. S3a), that λ1 at 220 nm and λ2 at 285 nm. Then, as the chemical bonds and functional groups on 4-DEAP shown in FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S3b), 4-DEAP has two absorption bands, namely the asymmetric stretching vibration (νas N-H) and the symmetric stretching vibration (νs N-H) of the N–H bond of the primary amine near 3469 and 3370 cm−1, confirming that the nitro group has been reduced to an amino group. And the HNMR (CDCl3) spectrum is shown in the Fig. S4. As shown in Fig. S5, OVA has successfully linked to 4-DEAP, which proved that coating antigen has been successfully prepared.

The TEM and DLS pictures in the Fig. 1 a indicated the R-CDs are monodispersed nanoparticles with diameter of around 2 nm. R-CDs optical qualities are often assessed using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, as well as fluorescence excitation and emission spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of R-CDs reveal substantial absorption throughout the visible band, as illustrated in Fig. 1b. Similar to literature reports, the peaks at 272 and 282 nm were examined respectively, owing to the π − π* transitions of C = C and C = N bonds in carbon cores in the sp2 C domain. While it has a dominating band at 550 nm, which may be derived from n → π* transition of many C − N/C = N or C − O/C = O structures. On the other hand, the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 1b) represents the optimal excitation/emission are about 550 nm/615 nm, respectively. Importantly, the fluorescence excitation spectrum and the primary absorption band of the R-CDs has been closely overlapped, indicating that nitrogen- and oxygen-related surface structures and states play a role in intense red emission.

The EDS spectrum (Fig. 1c) showed that the red carbon dots contain C, N, and O three elements. As Fig. 1d shown, the chemical bonds and functional groups on the R-CDs characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy. There are peaks at 3414 cm−1 and 3204 cm−1, indicating the existence of O–H and N–H bonds, respectively. Furthermore, C=N/C=O, C–N, and C–O expansion and contraction vibration are assigned to the bands at 1640 cm−1, 1380 cm−1, 1289 cm−1, and 1221 cm−1, respectively. Following that, Raman spectrum was used to analyze the structure of R-CDs. The Raman spectrum shows two bands at 1343 cm−1 (D band) and 1572 cm−1 (G band) (Fig. 1e). The D-band is created by sp3-hybridized carbon atoms derived from defects and oxygen-containing functional group covalently bonded to the graphemic structure, whereas the G band is caused by the graphitized structure's sp2 carbon network. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern in the Fig. 1f of R-CDs exhibits evident peak at around 27° corresponding to the (002) plane of the graphite, confirming the formation of R-CDs with a graphitic structure. As shown in Fig. S6a, the wide XPS spectra reveals three characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.1 eV, and 530.7 eV, showing that the R-CDs sample contains C, N, and O elements, respectively. In the high-resolution spectra of C 1 s (Fig. S6b), it can be decomposed into five binding energies. The energy peaks of 284.5 eV, 285.2 eV and 285.8 eV are caused by C–C/C=C, C–N and C–O bonds, respectively. C=N/C=O and –COOH bond formed energy peaks at 287.3 eV and 289.3 eV. Moreover, N of the three binding energies can be transformed from the high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1 s, namely pyridinic N (398.5 eV), pyrrolic N (399.7 eV) and graphitic N (400.7 eV) (Fig. S6c). Meanwhile, the high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s can be divided into two forms of O (Fig. S6d). For example, the energy peak at 530.6 eV is derived from the C=O bond, and the peak of the C-O bond is at 532.1 eV. The PL decay indicated that average lifetime of R-CDs was 2.55 ns under 550 nm excitation (Fig. S7), which was fitted by a single-exponential formula. The absolute quantum yields of R-CDs was measured to be 15.44% under the optimal excitation light of 550 nm via used an integrating sphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon dots coupled antibody
ce:section.ce:para: In the UV–vis spectroscopy (Fig. 2 a), due to its own structure, the R-CDs has absorption band in the 280 nm which overlapped with the characteristic absorption peak of anti-DEP Ab. Then, the absorption peak at 280 nm is enhanced when the R-CDs are coupled to the antibody successfully. What’s more, a new absorption band near 320 nm, it may because of the interaction between the R-CDs and the antibody. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of carbon dots were 550/610 nm, respectively. After coupling antibody, the optimal emission wavelength produced a slight blue shift, it may because Schiff base broaden the sp2 energy gap and cause “inverse” PL shift [41], which proved the successful prepared R-CDs-anti-DEP Ab (Fig. 2b). As shown in the Fig. 2c, we find that the intensity of peak at 1632 cm−1 had weakened, and where is a new band had formed at the 1685 cm−1, which means that R-CDs has successfully conjugated with anti-DEP antibody.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theory of fluorescent immunoassay based on R-CDs as labels
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 briefly expressed the illustration and principle of the R-CDs as probe used in the fluorescent immunoassay. Above all, the anti-DEP Ab coupling with the R-CDs as the fluorescent immunoprobe. As the Scheme 1a shown, when R-CDs are labeled with anti-DEP antibody, the red fluorescence is quenched. It may be because the solvent effect promotes the non-radiative relaxation transition and formation new hydrogen bonds, resulting the red fluorescence quenched [42–44]. The antibodies labeled R-CDs could recognize and specifically combine with the immobilized antigen. Then, added of the formamide, the fluorescence of R-CDs was turned on. Solvents have been proven in studies to cause electron transport on the surface of carbon dots, affecting their fluorescence [45–47]. Thus, R-CDs turn on fluorescence after adding formamide, it may due to the electron charge transfer on the surface of the red carbon dots and the change of the electron surface caused by the solvent. Firstly, the coating antigen (4-DEAP-OVA) on a 96-well microplate overnight. Among anti-DEP Ab, coating antigen and DEP molecules in the solution, the competitive immunoreaction was occurred. The coating antigen and samples containing DEP competitively reacted with the antibodies on the R-CDs, which well resulting in less amount specific binding of R-CDs labeled antibodies and coating antigen. In this competitive immunoassay, the concentration of DEP in the solution is the crucial factor of change of the fluorescence intensity.

Condition were optimized for achieving good analytical performance of the fluorescent immunoassay. The concentration of the coating antigen and R-CDs anti-DEP Ab, immunoreaction time, pH value and incubation time were the experimental parameters effected the sensitivity of the fluorescent immunoassay, that which were systematically optimized. The fluorescence intensity was aroused by the specific reactions between antigen and antibody. In this project, the concentration of coating antigen was diluted at the range of 10–80 µg/mL and the antibody was diluted at the range of 1.35–32.4 µg/mL. According the specific antigen–antibody recognition (Fig. 3 a), the optimum concentration of coating antigen and R-CDs labeled antibody were 30 µg/mL and 21.6 µg/mL, respectively. Above all, the different immunoreaction time (0.5 h–3 h) was investigated to achieve the best immunoreaction. The fluorescence intensity achieved maximum when the immunoreaction time was 2 h (Fig. 3b). Therefore, 2 h was selected as the immune response time for establishing competitive immunoassay. What’s more, the pH variation of the PBS buffer (5.5–10.0) and the block time (0.5–2.0 h) were investigated to optimum the principal conditions of fluorescence immunoassay. When the blocking duration increased to 1 h, the fluorescence intensity reached equilibrium, as seen in Fig. 3c and d, and the highest difference fluorescence value was obtained when the pH value was 7.4. Based on the results, the optimized block time and pH value for the getting the highest sensitivity were determined to be 1 h and 7.4, respectively.

In our research, we employed the R-CDs conjugated anti-DEP Ab in developing a direct competitive fluorescence immunoassay. For the direct competitive immunoassay, coating antigen and R-CDs labeled antibody concentrations are the crucial factors that determined the sensitivity of fluorescence immunoassay. With the optimum requirements, the standard curves were obtained for the detection of DEP. High sensitivity immunoassay was successfully developed using R-CDs as labels with fluorescence signal output. The fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs labeled anti-DEP Ab system was determined in the presence of a range of concentration of DEP standard, as displayed in Fig. 4 a. The fluorescence intensity steadily reduced as the analyte concentration increased. The fluorescence intensity and analyte concentration were found to have a good linear relationship. The linear equation was F = 8398.47–1853.61 × LogC (where the correlation coefficient, R2 = 0.998). The detecting limit (LOD 3σ/k, in which σ is the standard errors of the blank and k is the slope within the dynamic range) was 0.0056 ng/mL. The analytic property of fluorescence immunoassay was obviously improved compared to the HRP-based conventional ELISA (LOD was 0.019 ng/mL) as the Fig. 4b showed.

To verity the specificity of the proposed immunosensor, the competitors, including Dibutyl phthalate (DBP), Dioctyl phthalate(DOP), Dimethyl phthalate (DMP), Dicyclohexyl phthalate (DCHP), were investigated by our proposed fluorescent immunoassay. According the Table S1, we find that this proposed immunosensor has excellent specificity, which illustrated the competitors cause no obvious impact on our proposed fluorescent immunoassay, indicating that our proposed fluorescent immunoassay has excellent selectivity toward DEP.

We measured the DEP in the samples to illustrate the ability of our sensing system in the investigated samples. To ensure accuracy, we employed test samples with DEP concentrations of 0.05, 0.1, and 0.5 μg/mL. Real samples spiked with DEP standard solution at various concentrations were tested in parallel by this developed FIA and GC-MS/MS (referenced the National Standard of the People's Republic of China GB/T 37860-2019, GB 31604.30-2016) to further verify the project's dependability and correctness (Table 1 ). As the results showed, the recoveries (n = 3) ranged from 96.0% to 115.1% with the relative standard deviations less than 8.14% for FIA. GC-MS/MS with the recoveries ranging from 93.4% to 113.9% (n = 3), and the relative standard deviation less than 5.28%. Compared with GC-MS/MS, the fluorescence immunoassay method we established has a wide linear range (5 × 10−2 ng/mL-10 ng/mL) and low detection limit (0.0056 ng/mL), so the sample recovery was determined using a 100-fold dilution of the original sample. Because the arithmetic means of the measurements ‾x (relative standard deviation RSD = s /‾x × 100%) using this FIA method is from a 100-fold diluted sample, the RSD is larger using this FIA method than GC-MS/MS. The results indicated that the proposed approach consistent with results of GC-MS/MS, which proof that the proposed FIA method is beneficial for the determination of lower and trace components of DEP in the real samples. Meanwhile, the precision of the established method was estimated by the relative standard deviation of the intra-and inter-assay recovery. As shown in Table S2, the intra-assay recovery rate ranged from 95.1% to 115.1% with the relative standard deviation less than 8.14%, while the inter-assay recovery rate ranged from 99.7% to 108.2% with the relative standard deviation less than 6.25%. These results proved the accuracy and reliability of the developed fluorescent turn-on immunoassay for the detection of DEP in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical properties about the competitive immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: Compared to other reported strategies (Table 2 ), this proposed immunoassay has shown a higher sensitivity, which has lower LOD (0.0056 ng/mL) and wider linear detection range (5 × 10−2 ng/mL–10 ng/mL). Our developed fluorescence immunoassay has the advantages of simple, rapid and specific detection of DEP. R-CDs-based fluorescent signal displayed the benefits of good biocompatibility, easy synthesis, and simple embellishment compared with organic fluorescent dye and QDs. More importantly, it is more stability than classic the enzyme-based ELISA. Based on these excellences, our proposed fluorescent immunoassay can be used as a substitute for conventional colorimetric ELISA in detection of DEP in the real samples.

In this study, we developed a practical and novel fluorescence immunoassay for detecting environmental endocrine DEP in daily consumables samples. Based on R-CDs as the signal probe, it was turned on by formamide. Compared with traditional enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays, the R-CDs-based fluorescent immunoassay could significantly enhance the sensitivity due to fluorescence intensity is not affected by the external environment. On the other hand, the R-CDs can directly label antibody without extra embellishment, which will provide fast and easily synthetic procedure. Despite this, the suggested approach demonstrated a wide DEP linear range (5 × 10−2 ng/mL–10 ng/mL) and highly sensitivity (LOD was 0.0056 ng/mL). Furthermore, this novel type of fluorescence immunoassay exhibited excellent precision, accuracy and specificity. In a word, this strategy could be extended as an effective protocol for monitoring the concentration of DEP in the real samples.

Biru Chen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Lei Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Yue Hu: Methodology, Data curation. Bolin Liu: Methodology. Ming Guo: Formal analysis, Resources. Qi Zhang: Software, Resources. Qianqian Yang: Investigation. Mingcui Zhang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21175004) and the Anhui provincial Natural Science Foundation (2108085MB68).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107350.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A monograph on the remediation of hazardous phthalates

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Benjamin, S. Pradeep, M.S. Josh, S. Kumar, E. Masai, A monograph on the remediation of hazardous phthalates, J. Hazard. Mater., 298 (2015) 58-72. https://doi.rog/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2015.05.004
